next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 15

The shift happened on a Tuesday morning in late April, though Gavin only worked out that it was Tuesday afterward, checking the class schedule he'd stopped following. He was walking to breakfast through the third-floor corridor when he noticed that the Permanent Sticking Charm on the portrait of Barnabas the Barmy was failing along its lower-left edge.

Not the portrait itself, not the frame. The agreement beneath the charm — the substrate commitment someone had made centuries ago, probably a long-dead Hogwarts custodian who'd wanted the painting to stay put — was thinning where it anchored to the stone. Old mortar crumbling between bricks, the structure sound but the joints going. A decade more and Barnabas would start to swing.

He kept walking. He was three steps past the portrait before he understood what had just happened.

He hadn't translated. He hadn't seen the portrait and then reached back through the notation he'd memorized, matching the visible effect to a substrate grammar, working out what was there the way he'd been doing for months — slow, effortful, one concept at a time, like reading a foreign language with a dictionary open beside the text. He'd just seen it. The agreement, the thinning, the timescale. All of it at once, the way you saw a word in English and knew what it meant without sounding out the letters.

He stopped in the corridor. A seventh-year Ravenclaw carrying an armful of textbooks so tall she had to tilt her chin to see over them edged past with a muttered "excuse me," and Gavin stepped aside without looking at her. His hands w...
open_hooks_to_carry: The detention for missing homework: writing lines while mentally solving ward-substrate problems. The comedy of scale played deadpan — OWLs as irrelevant as ant governance, but he is fifteen and his handwriting is bad
Miren's confrontation: not casual but the real, friendship-ending kind. She sees a boy who used a book as an excuse to stop being present. She is not wrong about the observable facts
prev_tail_excerpt:
...he hadn't started and would fail because he didn't have time for Potions essays anymore, because what he had time for was a manuscript designed for a dead civilization that contained maintenance instructions he couldn't follow for a structure he wasn't capable of repairing.

He went down the stairs toward the barrel entrance. The corridor near the kitchens smelled like bread, warm and specific and alive. Through a doorway he caught a glimpse of a house-elf carrying a platter of sausages, small and serious-faced, absorbed in the work of feeding people.

The barrel entrance was ahead. He could hear voices inside — Hufflepuffs getting ready for the day, the sounds of a common room doing what common rooms did in the morning.

He tapped the barrel in the right rhythm and went in. The warmth came first — the fire up, the room heated, the contrast with the tower so sharp that his face prickled and his hands began to ache as the blood came back. Students at tables, eating, talking. A sixth-year was grumbling about a Herbology assignment. Someone laughed at something someone else had said. A copy of the *Daily Prophet* lay on a side table, its headline about the war — always about the war.

He went to his dormitory and changed into dry robes. His fingers were clumsy with the buttons. His left hand shook. He dressed and picked up his Charms textbook and his wand — his wand, which he kept forgetting he had — and went to class.

The day began. The wards arced overhead, beautiful and failing, holding and thinning. The world went on beneath them, small and loud and precious, not knowing.
state_deltas_required: The absurd gap between Gavin's actual work and his institutional life — failing OWLs while grappling with planetary defense, writing lines for missed Herbology homework while modeling ward-substrate resonance — colliding with Miren's final confrontation about his withdrawal

Relational — Gavin and Miren's friendship breaks. Neither is wrong. The break comes from the impossibility of shared ground when one person's reality is invisible to the other. This is the novel's emotional climax in the human register
